title: Vehicle–canine collaboration for urban pipeline methane leak detection

data_scope.study_type: primary field measurement campaign (no public geospatial datasets reused)
data_scope.leak_prone_components: underground steel pipelines and aboveground risers most prone
data_scope.campaign_date_range: not explicitly stated in available metadata

core_conclusion: A two-stage vehicle-canine collaboration (VCC) strategy improves urban methane leak detection: vehicle-mounted sensors first screen the network to flag candidate leak areas, then sniffer canines pinpoint exact release points. Across ~4,000 km of distribution pipeline in 20 Chinese cities, canines localized 432 release sources within 220 vehicle-flagged leak areas, with underground steel pipelines and aboveground risers most leak-prone and leak density varying notably between cities.
why_it_matters: Protects the actual climate benefit of coal-to-gas transitions by catching offsetting methane leaks; combines wide vehicle reach with point-precise canine localization; field-validated at city scale (20 cities, ~4,000 km); targets explosion/public-safety hazards; prioritizes inspection by mapping which components and cities fail most.
why_nature: Couples urban infrastructure to climate and public safety in one operational strategy; large real-world 20-city, ~4,000 km empirical campaign with generalizable evidence; directly translatable to utility inspection protocols and component-replacement priorities; a deployable solution rather than only a diagnosis.
added_value_to_my_research: Explainable leak-risk model from 432 sources -> risk-targeted vehicle routing
Fuse bottom-up leak census with top-down remote sensing -> reconcile under-reported city methane
Recast leak localization as 2SFCA detection-catchment siting of vehicle/canine teams
MGWR/GWR on inter-city leak heterogeneity -> spatially non-stationary risk drivers
Agent-based + Monte Carlo stress test of VCC coverage under shocks and failures
